data.title: SmartCentres Offers A Walmart-Backed High Dividend Yield
data.description:
Introduction

A few years ago, I quite liked the relationship between SmartCentres (OTCPK:CWYUF) ( SRU.UN:CA) and its largest tenant, Walmart (WMT). Walmart represents in excess of 23% of SmartCentres’ rental revenue, with other grocery-related tenants representing an additional 7% of the rental income. Throwing in some other high-traffic stores like a Canadian Tire, Winners and Dollarama, and about 40% of the total rental revenue is generated from what I would consider to be reliable tenants.

Looking back at the AFFO performance

One of the elements I appreciate most about Canadian REITs is the fact that the AFFO calculation includes the sustaining capex required to keep the properties in good shape. Before discussing the AFFO result, it makes sense to have a look at the FFO first. As you can see below, the REIT had a pretty strong final quarter of 2024 wherein it reported a total FFO of C$101.4M after making some adjustments. That being said, if we would just look at the normalized FFO before taking certain non-recurring elements into account, we see the FFO decreased by almost 10% to just C$96.6M.

That’s a pity as the first few quarters of the year were quite strong, and on a full-year basis, the REIT’s FFO (before adjustments) increased by just under 0.5% to C$402.5M. The main reason for this can be found in the cost of debt. Whereas SmartCentres recorded a net interest expense of C$144M in FY 2023, this increased to C$169M in FY 2024. The weighted average interest rate on the secured debt was 3.97% while the average cost of the unsecured debt was 3.84%.

The weaker FFO on a reported basis also had a negative impact on the AFFO result. As you can see below, the AFFO decreased from C$92.2M to C$85M in the final quarter of 2024, while the full-year AFFO increased by almost 1.5% to C$359.4M.

The AFFO adjusted for the elements shown above was slightly lower on a full-year basis (but higher in the final quarter of last year). SmartCentres currently pays a monthly distribution of C$0.1542, and with a full-year AFFO of C$2.02 and C$1.91 per share after taking some of the adjustments into consideration, the distribution is covered, although the payout ratio remains high.

At the end of 2024, the book value per share was just over C$30, underpinned by the real estate assets which were valued at a discount rate of 6.53%. Also, keep in mind, the balance sheet contains approximately C$1.72B in properties under development, which of course are not yet contributing to the rental income.

Where will growth come from?

As the most recent debt issue by SmartCentres was a 6.5-year unsecured note with a 4.737% coupon, I think we should expect the total net interest expenses to increase further as cheaper debt securities will have to be refinanced with more expensive capital as SmartCentres is still working through its debt refinancing cycle. The proceeds of the new unsecured note were used to repay a C$160M 3.556% unsecured debt security, while later this year a 1.74% C$350M unsecured debt security will have to be refinanced. Adding 250-300 bps to the cost of debt to refinance the latter will likely cause the interest expenses to increase by approximately C$10M. Applying an average cost of debt of 4.25% to the C$5B in gross debt would result in C$212M in interest expenses, resulting in an increase of approximately C$50M per year.

This will, of course, weigh on SmartCentres’ financial results, but there are three reasons why I think the REIT should be able to fully absorb the higher interest expenses.

First of all, it will be able to hike the rent its tenants pay based on the inflation rate. Assuming an average increase in the net rental income by 2% per year would increase the NOI by C$11M per year. This means that, based on the current asset base, three years further down the road, about C$33M of the anticipated C$50M interest expense increase will be covered by (relatively low) rent hikes.

Secondly, the company is working on the completion of its development portfolio. As you can see below, the REIT should deliver some growth in its self-storage division, followed by the completion of residential real estate in 2027.

This will allow it to generate additional income and/or recycle some of its capital investments in the development space.

And finally, it’s perhaps important to emphasize about 69% of its portfolio is up for re-leasing in the next five years. As you can see below, SmartCentres has highlighted the current average market rent is approximately $29.9 per square foot versus the $23.48 per square foot in the expiring contracts.

A lot will depend on the negotiations, and I’m not convinced we can apply a 1:1 rent increase as SmartCentres may have to give some tenants a break in order to keep the traffic high and occupancy ratio robust, but even a $2.5/sq.ft. rent increase across the portfolio would result in a total additional rental income of around C$50M.

Additionally, some vacant properties were recently re-leased to new tenants.

Investment thesis

So while the higher interest rates will definitely weigh on the REIT’s financial results, I'm quite confident SmartCentres has enough aces up its sleeve to mitigate the impact.

It had been a few years since I looked at SmartCentres as I wasn’t a big fan of the relatively high payout ratio for its dividend. The payout ratio remains high, but the distributions are covered while I also like the REIT’s expansion plans and the recent announcement by Walmart to expand its operations in Canada. I think it's reasonable to assume SmartCentres will be able to benefit from this, as it is already one of Walmart’s main landlords in Canada. And as SmartCentres was recently able to sign up Costco (COST) as a tenant, perhaps it can build out a successful relationship with them as well.

I currently have no position in SmartCentres, but as I'm increasing my exposure to Canadian REITs, I will definitely keep an eye on SmartCentres as an income idea.

data.title: TSMC Q1: Excellent Results, Strong Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Article Thesis

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) aka TSMC reported excellent quarterly earnings results for the first quarter, showing it continues to benefit from broad AI tailwinds and its strong market position. The outlook for the current quarter is very positive as well and way better than what Wall Street was forecasting. Add an undemanding valuation, and this high-growth company looks like a Strong Buy – despite the geopolitical risks.

Past Coverage

I have written about Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in January, around one quarter ago. I was bullish on TSMC back then – with the stock pulling back since then due to the broad market decline and with TSM reporting very strong results for the first quarter, I am even more bullish today.

What Happened?

On Thursday morning, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company reported its first-quarter earnings results. The headline numbers can be seen in the following screencap from Seeking Alpha:

We see that Taiwan Semiconductor did not deliver a double beat this time, but results still looked pretty good – the earnings per share consensus estimate was beaten by 3%, while the revenue miss was smaller than 1%. For a company that grows its revenues by way above 30% in US Dollars (growth was even higher in Taiwan's currency), I do not believe that a sub-1% revenue miss is very telling. Earnings per share are more important anyway – the bottom line counts, after all – and here the beat was relatively pronounced.

Markets reacted positively to these results, with TSMC trading up by around 3% at the time of writing. They could be volatile in the near term, however.

TSMC: Strong Results, Tariffs Don't Seem To Be A Threat

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is the leading foundry on the globe; thus, it is not surprising that it benefits when chip markets are strong. That undoubtedly is the case, mainly thanks to the very strong demand that AI chip companies such as Nvidia (NVDA) experience. Nvidia is one of TSMC's core customers – TSMC thus benefits a lot when Nvidia is seeing strong demand and rising sales.

While AI is a huge demand driver for TSM, the company also benefits from other macro themes. The company also experienced appealing growth in some non-AI areas, such as automotive. With automobiles becoming ever more "techy," demand for computing power rises – and with autonomous driving tech still in the early innings, there is a lot of potential for future growth in this area, I believe.

On a quarter-to-quarter basis, TSMC saw its revenues decline in the smartphone market, but that isn't a surprise – for smartphones and other consumer goods, the fourth quarter is especially strong, due to the holiday impact in that quarter. When we look at the Q4-to-Q1 performance, the comparison for these consumer-centric markets is thus a tough one, which explains why TSMC saw its smartphone revenues decline. The same can be expected in all future years – Q1 will almost always be weaker than the previous Q4 when it comes to smartphone revenues.

While a strong performance for the first quarter wasn't too surprising – the company gives monthly sales results, after all – the company's Q2 outlook was a major surprise. Many feared that the current tariffs and trade tensions would hurt Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, but that does not seem to be the case:

The company guides for $28.4 billion to $29.2 billion in revenue for the current quarter, with a guidance midpoint of $28.8 billion. The company did $20.6 billion in revenue in last year's second quarter, which means that the current guidance implies a revenue growth rate of 39.8%. That is not only better than the growth rate during the first quarter, but it also is a very high growth rate in general for the company – this doesn't suggest that TSMC is feeling a lot of pressure from tariffs. When we consider that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company tends to outperform its own guidance – it did so in the first quarter as well, beating it by $130 million – there is, I believe, a solid chance that the actual growth rate will surpass 40%. But even if that is not the case, a high-30s growth rate for a company as large as TSMC is outstanding.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company's gross margin stood at 58.8%, which was above the guidance midpoint (TSMC tends to be conservative, as mentioned above) and which is highly attractive in absolute terms. TSMC's high gross margin, despite a manufacturing-heavy business model, is the result of strong pricing power. After all, as the global leader in its industry, TSMC can set prices that its customers basically have to take if they want advanced chips. For the current quarter, TSMC guides for a gross margin of around 58% – but that was the guidance for Q1 as well, and the company performed better, so I wouldn't be surprised if the same happens this quarter.

Looking at TSMC's cash flow generation, things were good during the first quarter as well. The company generated operating cash flows of around NT$626 billion, which is equal to a little more than $19 billion. Annualized, that is close to $80 billion, which is not only very impressive in absolute terms, but which is also massive for a company that is currently valued at "just" $680 billion. After all, if the rest of the year looks like Q1, TSMC would trade at an operating cash flow multiple of less than 9, or an operating cash flow yield of more than 11%. The company continues to invest heavily in its operations, of course, in order to benefit from the ongoing strong market growth rate in AI chips, but even after accounting for around $10 billion of capital expenditures, TSMC's free cash flows looked quite good, at around $9 billion – or around $36 billion annualized, which results in a free cash flow yield of well north of 5% despite TSMC's heavy investments.

TSMC: Undemanding Valuation, Some Macro Risks

Based on forecasted earnings per share of $9.07 during the current year, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company trades for 16.7x forward net profits. For a very fast-growing company that is an industry leader, that is not a high valuation at all, I believe. When we consider that TSMC beat the Q1 consensus and that it has a good chance of beating the Q2-Q4 consensus estimates as well – its guidance for Q2 was way stronger than expected, and it has a history of outperforming expectations, with more than 20 earnings per share beats in a row – its profits during the current year could easily come in north of $9.07, which would naturally mean that its "true" earnings multiple would be even lower than 16.7.

TSMC also looks quite inexpensive based on its enterprise value to EBITDA multiple:

The EV/EBITDA ratio, which accounts for debt and cash on the balance sheet, stands at just 9.7 right now – the lowest in recent memory, and way below the 5-year median at 14.1. To me, this suggests that TSMC is both quite cheap in absolute terms and quite cheap compared to how this high-quality growth player was valued in the past.

There are some geopolitical risks, mainly the potential for an escalation in the Taiwan conflict, but judging by TSMC's guidance, trade tensions and tariffs do not seem to be a problem, so the recent sell-off seems way overdone. For those that can stomach the Taiwan risk (which is, I believe, existent, but not overly large), TSMC looks very attractive right here – the company combined excellent growth, a strong outlook, a very compelling industry position, and a very undemanding valuation.

data.title: HubSpot: Fundamentals Are Behind Its Valuation
data.description:
Despite strong revenue growth in the past, I expect Hubspot (NYSE:HUBS) to experience a stagnation in growth and be challenged against the market leader in customer relationship management - Salesforce (CRM), which boasts significantly larger revenue, market cap, and higher-value enterprise clients. Hubspot's focus on the SMB [small & medium business] market and reliance on acquisitions for growth are diminishing the future return on investment and profitability potential for the company.

Ultimately, I rate the company a “hold” as it has downside protection as a potential acquisition target from Salesforce competitors.

A possible reversal tailwind to keep track of is a rejuvenation of the SMB economy that was severely pressured in the last few years.

Hubspot vs Salesforce: It Pays To Be A Leader

Salesforce has 14x more revenues than Hubspot, trades at 9x the market cap compared to HUBS, has the leading market share, and more valuable clientele [higher-margin enterprise customers] versus HUBS.

Relative to sales, HUBS is also more expensive than CRM as it trades at 8.7x EV/Revenue [forward CY’25], compared to CRM’s 6x. However, the confounding factor here is that HUBS is estimated to grow at close to double the rate of CRM [and keep it up for the next few years], while CRM is expected to recover and maintain revenue growth rates around 10%.

Despite HUBS being able to outgrow CRM in the next few years, my view is that the type of customers CRM has [enterprise-level companies] have larger purchasing power and offer investors larger monetization potential. Hubspot has put itself in a tighter corner as its customers are primarily SMB which have more limited budgets, and can expand their spend based on their prospective growth levels. In essence, betting on HUBS is betting on the recovery of the economy for smaller businesses. Thus far, this category has been severely punished by the market as the value has accrued to the larger companies that keep consolidating the industry.

This is not to say that HUBS is a bad investment, but that the KPI for investors keeping HUBS in their portfolio should be the potential rejuvenation of the SMB and mid-market sector where the company holds some 3.4% market share. It is also a diversification component as CRM does not [and should not] bother capturing this market segment, hence making HUBS an addition to a portfolio that wants exposure to the software customer relationship market.

How Big Is The Customer Relationship Market

The customer relationship market size is estimated [1, 2, 3] to be between $76B and $101B, with a 2024 midpoint estimate of $89B. From this, CRM holds some 43%, and HUBS has about 3.4%. Peers in the space include Salesforce, Hubspot, Adobe [Marketo], Intuit’s MailChimp, Pipedrive, ActiveCampaign, Zoho, etc. The exuberance cycle in customer relationship management is slowly stabilizing and investors are now able to more reliably forecast growth, leading to a re-rating in equity values. The industry kicked-off with CRM as the pioneer, and had many startups that attempted to carve out a market slice in the last 10-years. So much so, that in my view, every other startup pitch was about creating a CRM software of some sort. However, we now see that trend slowing down as established players have captured key customers and built their feature moat. For this reason, I believe that the industry is entering its mature growth stage, and going forward I will be forecasting growth rates up to 15% for peers in this space.

A subsection of this market is known as customer engagement market [CEM], i.e. marketing automation and lead generation. This is HUBS’s key segment, where the company competes directly and has an 8% share of the estimated $38.4B total market. Separating these CRM and CEM markets is blurry, as many of the products companies offer lay somewhere in the customer relationship chain. So it will not be a stretch for peers to develop features that are used in different phases of the sales cycle.

Growth Vitals Are Stagnating

Thus far, HUBS’s operating profit has been negative. This is understandable as the company is engaged in growth investments. However, it is somewhat unsettling that the kind of growth levers the company is pulling aren’t purely organic, but it has leapt forward in its development cycle and moved to growth via acquisitions.

Hubspot has spent $40M for acquiring Cacheflow and $142M for APIHub [p. 81] in 2024 and 2023 respectively; and this year, HUBS spent $60M to acquire Frame AI [p. 91]. Typically, I view acquisitions as a great way to drive growth for companies that are at a mature growth stage, but are a risky play when the business itself is unprofitable. Growth via acquisitions indicates a lack of technological investment opportunities, and means that the company can’t entice market participants to switch to their products via superiority, but must buy out smaller peers in order to capture a market share large enough to build a stable moat.

Additionally, Hubspot’s average subscription revenue per customer is stagnating, which indicates that the company is likely pricing its products high, leading to smaller competitors recognising the opportunity to move in. Existing customers seem to be favoring lower-end subscriptions [p. 51] as they mitigate their SaaS spend. During my research of HUBS, the general sentiment from people reviewing the product is that HUBS is steeply priced with the pricing reflecting a lack of viable alternatives for customers, not the value they feel they get for features.

Essentially, HUBS now has to fend off disruptors, and is left with growing revenue by focusing on increasing the number of customers. For this reason, I think that growth vitals are showings signs of stagnation, and will be forecasting a decelerating growth rate in my modeling – as opposed to analysts which estimate that growth picks up after 2025.

The Profitability Question

After years of operating in the red, HUBS is on the cusp of breaking even at the business level. For FY’24 GAAP operating margin was -2.6%, compared to -9.3% in 2023. Non-GAAP operating margin was 17.5%, compared to 15.5% in 2023. The company has a non-GAAP op. margin target of 20% to 22% by 2027, indicating that GAAP profitability will likely be lower.

Investing in HUBS carries with it the implied assumption that the company will be able to execute on profitability once growth stabilizes.

In my view, the company has a longer road to achieving the profitability margins it has set in its earnings presentation and investors will be footing the stock-based compensation dilution for a significant period before seeing a return on their investment. This isn’t necessarily bad, it just means that investors entering the company at this stage are seeding their investment, but need to be prepared to wait for some time before the reaping phase arrives.

Hubspot Is Still Richly Priced

The 8.7x forward EV to revenue multiple is breaking below historical levels, which is a good sign if you believe the company can maintain double digit growth rates.

In my view, the company is about to break into profitability which will be a positive catalyst for the stock, but I also expect it to re-rate lower gradually as the market realizes that the high revenue growth rates may not be sustainable.

My target multiple for Hubspot is 5x EV/Revenue by 2028 [end of 2027], for which I project 14%, 12%, and 10% revenue growth rates respectively, leading to a forward revenue of $3.7B and an enterprise value of $18.5B.

The company has good cash balance management, which has led to $2B in reserves, of which I will net out $1B in order to reconcile EV to market cap. This leads to my estimated market value of $17.5B. HUBS has a diluted share count of 51.2M, which I expect to grow at a 4.3% CAGR, leading to a 58M share count by 2028. This leads to a forward pricing of $300 per share, significantly lower than the current $548 price. Discounting the forward pricing back to today at a 8% cost of equity, I get a present value of $14B or $240 per share.

Conversely, if you retain the assumption that the company should trade at 9x revenues, then the resulting forward value would be $33.3B or $574 per share. Discounted at a 8% rate, it implies a pricing of $456 per share or $26B, roughly in-line with today’s price. In a sense, this is the current market model which investors can use as a comparison to their forecasts.

In situations like this, where a stock is priced ahead of its value, I would typically estimate the time it takes for the fundamentals to converge to value. However, in the case of HUBS I view the R/R asymmetry to be negative i.e. I expect more bad news than good over the next three years; and would rather wait for a better opening.

For the reasons above I rate the company as a “hold”, with the assumption that the market re-rates down to CRM’s multiples instead of holding the elevated pricing.

Acquisition Potential In A Downturn

In the event of a negative market catalyst, HUBS management may find itself trading as a value stock which would open the conversation of integrating HUBS within a competitor like MSFT, ORCL, or even CRM. For this reason, I feel that investors have some downside protection as the business would continue to be valuable even if negative headwinds stack up. In my view, selling the company while you are ahead is a much better position to be in, but no-one can really tell how things will turn in the future.

Note that this has to be a willing decision, as investors may find it difficult to force an event like this because of the company’s protective bylaw structure [p. 39, 2].

Risks

  • The recent drop in pricing may look like an opportunity, but even if so, it is likely still behind the fundamentals.
  • AI and industry consolidation may pose a risk to the amount of new digital services the company can pitch to customers. While AI is a convenient tool for search and text summarization; customer service AI may cheapen a product suite.
  • While its digital products have a lower pricing than Salesforce, they may not be as sticky as management would hope.

data.title: Capitalizing On Biotech Market Shifts
data.description:
Join ROTY Biotech today!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Daniel Snyder: Hey, everyone, Daniel Snyder here from Seeking Alpha. Today, we are going to be diving into the world of biotech, which I'm super excited about, especially within these crazy markets. I mean, obviously, as we're hosting this right now, April 16th, we saw Powell come out. There has been a quite - a move in the market right now. And well, that just seems to be the nature of things nowadays.

So obviously, when we focus on growth, we talk about biotech. We have Jonathan Faison from ROTY Biotech here today to talk to us. But first, let me go ahead and get a quick legal disclaimer out of the way.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Any views or opinions expressed in the webinar do not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha does not take account of your objectives or your financial situation and does not offer any personalized investment advice. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, U.S. investment adviser, or investment bank.

Information provided by the investing group does not constitute investing advice. Investing group leaders are third-party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body. Any content and tools on the platform are offered for information purposes only.

And with that out of the way, I would love to introduce to you all Jonathan. If you don't know who he is, well, now you do. He is the man that I'm like, if I have a question about biotech, if I have something about FDA, if I have a question about what drug might be cornering a market or what's the next potential, he is the one to come to. So I'm super excited that we were able to grab some time from him today.

So Jonathan, how are you doing?

Jonathan Faison: Hey, good. Good to be here. Thank you, again.

DS: All right. So we've got a good sizable crowd here hanging out with us today. And I'm sure there's going to be questions for you about this space because it's some sort of an unknown. Some people don't even like to touch biotech, right, when it comes to…

JF: For good reason, I completely understand it.

DS: So before we dive deeper into talking about the stocks that you want to tell people about today and talking about the lay of the land, why don't you go ahead and just give people a kind of a quick background of how you got into all of this?

JF: Okay. So I think most who follow know the story, but I started investing in 2008, had no idea what I was doing. This was right before the financial crisis, probably lost half of the life savings into it, made a bunch of poor calls along the way, every mistake you can imagine, started writing on Seeking Alpha, basically to share my ideas, to keep improving.

One thing I loved is that the comment section was brutal, especially when it comes to biotech. Doctors and others who obviously have a lot more background knowledge than I do in the sector were able to call me out where I got it wrong. That's how you get better as an investor. I never thought it would turn into a biotech service of any kind. I was just sharing what I was doing with my portfolio, what's worked along the years, very lucky to have survived my mistakes and tried to share what I've learned so other people don't have to go through the same roller coaster ride that I did. Though, as you said with Powell lately, there's kind of no avoiding that.

DS: I mean, it's a little bit of everything, right? You get the overall market dynamics. But what I like is that you dive into these actual companies. And we had a lot of conversations the other week as we were planning for this about what we wanted to talk about and something you mentioned to me at that moment in time that I hope you can address here, as you say, it's kind of not even a fundamental market right now. Could you elaborate more into that?

JF: Sure. So on the slides, I don't know if we bring them up, but it doesn't. Sure. But anyway, they have the XBI, you can see it's fallen from 105 down to 70 over, even this the last half year, the BBC, which is a clinical biotech ETF, that's also at lows, it’s not even when we thought it was double bottoming at the same levels as the 2023 lows, it completely plummeted below that as well. And we're seeing that spread to – throughout the sector, the baby being thrown with – thrown out with the bathwater.

If we go down to the third slide, let me pull this up. All right, I guess you're controlling it. There we go. Yeah, you can see the sector, the BBC is down 46% over the last three years. And it's no wonder people are steering clear of the sector. If we move to the next one.

So the perfect storm continues. You've had funds that are forced to sell at exactly the moment they don't want to. Investor redemptions forced. You have liquidations of companies trading below cash. Everyone's asking is the sector broken or is it just a bear market? Personally, to me, that sounds like capitulation.

You could say almost 50%, almost half of companies under $1 billion are trading under negative enterprise value, trading below their cash. There's this issue of zombie biotech companies, which also turns people off to the sector, completely get it. These companies where their lead drug fails, their next drugs in the pipeline, let's say, are preclinical or they have nothing and they'll in-license something preclinical. And instead of being good stewards of capital and the best option would be to return that money to shareholders and close down. Sutro Biopharma is a good example, STRO, where we talked about it in the last webinar. I wrote an article on it how I liked the technology, but I couldn't get behind the company because their lead construct was going after folate receptor alpha, which is a crowded target in the ADC world going after - they tried to claim management that there was white space in the low to medium expressors of the target.

But actually, NCCN guidelines, the approved drug from ImmunoGen, which was a holding of ours, that got bought out by AbbVie, I believe. They actually can address those populations with the Avastin combination. And so Sutro, long story short, after all this time of claiming that they were going to continue with this construct, they let the news loose that they are de-prioritizing, which means they're going to try to partner. Probably nobody wants that asset, and they're going to move forward some preclinical drug candidates instead.

And so the market, you're not looking at any meaningful catalysts then for the company potentially until those. You have to get them through IND-enabling studies, then you have to get them in Phase 1, slow dose escalation up. And so long story short, there’s a lot of people who would argue that the company is better off liquidating and returning the cash to shareholders.

Fortunately, in the news this morning, we saw a company – a couple of companies where funds are pushing to do that. And then, of course, the uncertainty from the Trump layoffs at the FDA, a lot of companies are asking whether the timelines are still intact for regulatory, if they've agreed on endpoints for certain studies, especially in the rare disease area where if we're waiting for longer-term outcomes, these studies could take forever.

So they're allowing for biomarker endpoints. Companies are wondering if that's still on the table, especially with Marks gone as well. So definitely, the uncertainty there as we were talking about Powell’s remarks today, unfortunately, there's this connotation that biotech – when the overall market is up, we're up more, but when it's down, we're down even more as well. And so it's definitely the highest volatility, highest risk sector.

DS: Yeah, I was going to mention on top of that, I mean, I was just pulling up XBI here, year-to-date down 17% for the sector from the XBI ETF. So some people look at this market environment. We've been talking about this lately in webinars that I'm sure the market's crazy, but there's actually these green shoots of opportunities.

JF: Sure.

DS: And even Steve was talking about the Quant system the other day, like one of the first Mag7 stocks has actually populated as a Strong Buy. And that was Amazon, if you wanted to go back and watch it. But we're starting to see the prices of stocks come down. So now we're all turned to the earnings and waiting for the earnings calls to come down the line and see what happens with management and forward guidance and everything else that everybody's worried about right now. How is that impacting biotech in general? I mean, they've…

JF: Sure.

DS: …their Capex – they spend, right? They spend, they go take the money for the loans. They're trying to do all these medical drugs, there's a lot of uncertainty. How are we supposed to invest here in this sector right now?

JF: Sure, if we can move to the next slide, and a shout out, I see Galzus Research in the comments as well for those who perhaps don't follow him. He does a lot of public articles on Seeking Alpha. I'm a big fan. I found him to be rather balanced. Balanced takes are hard to come by in biotech.

So again, our rule of getting in while that valuation is around 1x peak sales potential. If you have MDD for their - Auvelity, their first drug, plus let's say around 2 billion in peak sales for that second indication, that compares pretty favorably to the current valuation. So definitely, one I need to be revisiting as well.

DS: Very cool. I wanted to offer it up to you. Is there anything else you want to touch on right quick? I know we've been going for quite a while here. We covered our stocks that we’re going to cover out plus so much more. But I know…

JF: I'll do one last one just because I see there's so many of them guys there and I'm sorry I can't respond to everybody. And there's quite a few of those that I'm not well versed with. But like I said, the great thing is asking in chat.

I see Replimune there, R-E-P-L. I believe they have a June PDUFA date or RP1 and PD-1 failed melanoma. This is a simple injection that would be very easily combined with PD-1 for those patients who failed PD-1 as a frontline. And what's interesting is we own that one back in the day, we sold it for around 20% gain because I wasn't – anytime management companies push back timelines that can be a negative sign.

So we got out before they had the failed pivotal trial in CSCC. But what's interesting again is trying to manage that regulatory risk. Myself in the past, what I would have done is enter the stock ahead of this PDUFA date. Now my plan in Playbook, so in my phone, I have what is kind of like a Playbook, like in chess, you try to plan your next few moves ahead of time. And for me, that takes all the emotion out of it. So in Playbook, what I have written for Replimune is I'm going to wait for the PDUFA data. I'm not allowed to enter before.

If they get approved, I could buy a starter position. And then as launch metrics confirm that there is demand, I could round out, fill up the position and hold it for a multi-year launch. And it's nice that their technology could be applied to other areas outside of melanoma. They have some low-hanging fruit in other skin cancers. But again, this is a way that I manage risk personally. I'm just trying to prioritize these commercial stage setups that are already approved first and a few limited clinical stage where there's as many odds, factors in our favor as possible.

So I guess it's just a question of when you've gotten your hands burned on the stove so many times, you do everything possible to avoid that.

DS: I mean, it's true. It goes back to what you were saying, though, risk management. And if this is how you have to do it to manage your risk, more power to you, man. We all have our war stories here in these markets as well. But…

JF: 100%, 100%.

DS: I was going to say, let's go ahead and wrap it up here. We've been going for quite a while. Everyone encourage you enough, ROTY Biotech. Jonathan, you've been more than gracious with your time preparing for this and as well being here to present today. Can't thank you enough. It's an ever-changing market as we all know right now. And this space in particular with FDA approvals and clinical testing and how is AI going to disrupt and are they going to ban commercial, I mean, just crazy, crazy shakeups happening right now.

So again, thank you. I want to give you a quick second before we jump off. Is there anything that you would like to share or say to everybody here?

JF: Oh, no, not at all. Hope you'll give us a test spin. I also post some stuff on X, try to help people. You're more than welcome to private message me as well. Sometimes it can be intimidating in a larger group to put your question out there, even though we say there's no dumb questions, obviously, when you're either starting out, or you just don't feel like voicing in a group that you don't know particularly, we're a pretty tight knit group, everybody.

We've, as you said, we've had some war stories together. There's been, in my case, since I started the service and started investing for that matter, there's probably been at least 4 times where I was like in that stage, fetal position where you're about to throw in the towel. And then a veteran, somebody who's been in the market and the sector a lot longer than I was would pick me up, dust me off, tell me how I could improve.

So the only reason I'm still in the game is the people that helped me out there. So that's what we try to build in community is everybody helping everybody.

DS: Yeah, exactly. I mean, seriously, if you have a question, 14-day free trial, join, you ask the question, try to get your answer, and then you see how you go from there. But everyone, thank you so much for taking the time to hang out with us today.

If you're watching the replay, thank you so much for watching it as well. And I'm Daniel Snyder. This is Jonathan. We'll see you in the next webinar.

JF: Hey, thanks, Daniel. Thanks, everyone. Cheers.

data.title: Will Tariff-Related Inflation Derail The Bond Market's Rally?
data.description:
Rising US bond prices have offset the slide in US equities so far this year, but the outlook for fixed income may be more precarious than the current rear-window perspective suggests.

President Donald Trump put most of his tariffs on a 90-day pause and so the immediate effects of higher import prices have been curtailed to a degree. The question is whether the reprieve is temporary? Much depends on the success of the administration’s efforts to craft trade deals over the next several months. Whatever the outcome, the results will affect inflation, monetary policy and, by extension, the bond market.

Deciding what’s likely to unfold is unusually challenging at the moment because Trump’s agenda and decision-making process is a moving target and opaque to most outsiders. Meanwhile, the bond market is holding on to modest gains so far this year, offering diversification benefits to losses in stocks, based on a set of ETFs through Wednesday’s close (Apr. 16). The strongest performer: medium-term Treasuries (IEF), with a 3.7% year-to-date gain. That’s a handsome premium over the US investment-grade bond benchmark (BND), which is up 2.2% in 2025.

The losers this year for bonds are limited to lower-rated securities, led by Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN), which has shed 1.1% in 2025.

The outlook for bonds will likely be driven by how the tariff-related inflation plays out in the months ahead. Fed Chairman Powell offered some clues on the central bank’s current thinking. Speaking yesterday at the Economic Club of Chicago, he said:

The level of the tariff increases announced so far is significantly larger than anticipated. The same is likely to be true of the economic effects, which will include higher inflation and slower growth. Both survey- and market-based measures of near-term inflation expectations have moved up significantly, with survey participants pointing to tariffs. Survey measures of longer-term inflation expectations, for the most part, appear to remain well anchored; market-based breakevens continue to run close to 2 percent.

No change in monetary policy is expected at the moment, he added. “For the time being, we are well positioned to wait for greater clarity before considering any adjustments to our policy stance.”

The policy-sensitive US 2-year Treasury yield continues to trade near its lowest level in recent months, well below the current Fed funds target rate range (4.25%-4.50%) - a setup that suggests the market is still expecting a rate cut in the near future.

data.title: Hasbro: The Toymaker Faces Big Questions In Q1 As Tariff Risks Loom
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Hasbro, Inc. (NASDAQ:HAS) isn’t the type of stock I usually cover, and it may not be the most popular ticker among retail investors today. However, there is certainly a lot to say about this company right now, both because of its business and President Trump’s tariffs that most likely will hit Hasbro hard.

Hasbro is going through a visible transformation - shifting away from content production and re-centering itself around core toys, games, and licensing. While that strategy makes a lot of sense long-term, in the short term, it leaves investors with a complex mix of stabilizing fundamentals, serious macro risks, and capped growth expectations.

The stock price has strongly fallen over the past year, but in many ways, the story isn’t just about Hasbro as a business. The biggest risk right now is Trump’s tariff on China. Hasbro, same as its competitors like Mattel (MAT), heavily relies on Chinese manufacturing. I’m rating Hasbro as a Hold ahead of Q1 earnings (before market open on April 24th). There are signs of operational improvement, yes, but I believe the current setup doesn’t offer enough clarity or upside to justify opening a position right now. That said, if you’re already holding the stock for the dividend or waiting on the turnaround, I can understand sticking with it.

Company Overview

Hasbro is best known as one of the world’s largest toy and game companies, owning iconic names like Monopoly, Transformers, Nerf, and Peppa Pig, as well as running the massively successful Wizards of the Coast division, which includes Magic: The Gathering and Dungeons & Dragons (very popular tabletop games that now also generate revenue through digital and licensing channels). The company tried itself in entertainment (content production), and even acquired entertainment company eOne for 4 billion dollars, but decided to sell it four years later for only $500 million, taking huge losses.

The company’s Q4 2024 results showed signs that this decision to stop working on eOne is starting to pay off slowly. While total revenue for the quarter declined 15% year-over-year, most of that drop came from the divestiture of eOne. If we exclude that, the revenue decline was only 3%, and management emphasized improvement across some core segments of its business.

For example, Hasbro’s gaming and digital business delivered strong margins. It happened mostly thanks to licensing revenue from the rising popularity of its mobile game “Monopoly Go!”, which contributed $112 million for the full year. By the way, I’ve installed this game once, couldn’t get myself to stop playing, and deleted it because it was very time-consuming. Not saying it as a negative thing, quite the opposite, I usually delete only the games I like too much, so I assume the popularity of this one will only grow over time.

Adjusted operating margin for the quarter reached 10.2%, a significant year-over-year improvement driven by cost reductions, supply chain optimization, and a more favorable business mix. On a GAAP basis, the company reported a net loss of $0.25 per share due to restructuring and amortization charges, but on an adjusted basis, earnings came in at $0.46, beating expectations by $0.11.

Management also reported significant cost savings, with gross savings of $370 million and a 17% year-over-year reduction in inventory levels, a big improvement compared to the supply chain issues the company had in the past.

What I’ll Be Watching in Q1

The expectations for Q1 2025 earnings results are low. The consensus revenue estimate is only at $770 million, much lower than $1.1 billion in the previous quarter. Adjusted EPS is at $0.67. It’s important to note that analyst sentiment has turned slightly more cautious in recent weeks, with more downward than upward EPS and revenue revisions. That tells us the market is not positioned for a beat, which, in theory, makes it easier for Hasbro to surprise to the upside. But that depends on more than just cost-cutting. Besides, the stock price is now heavily correlated to any Trump decisions about tariffs on China, so even if the earnings come in positive, I can’t expect them to stop the overall downward trend of the HAS stock price.

I am really curious to see what happens with the Wizards of the Coast segment after these earnings. This segment has been the fastest and the most stable growing one, and given the fact that the company is investing heavily in it, I can assume the growth will continue. If it does, it will definitely be the reason for a more bullish outlook.

Its core toy segment, Consumer Products, is still declining, which is really unfortunate. Last quarter, we saw revenue drop 12% year-over-year in this department, and the operating margin was just 4.5%. Even after adjusting for one-time inventory costs, the segment is still under pressure from softer volumes and a challenging retail environment. If this business doesn’t show signs of stabilization, it will continue to weigh on the company’s overall results, and if the tariffs stick, margins could fall way lower than the 4.5% that we saw in Q4, which could turn this segment unprofitable. That probably won’t happen this quarter, or even in the next few, thanks to inventory buffering, but it’s definitely something to keep in mind.

The Biggest Risk: Tariffs

If you read until this point, you already realize that one of the most important macro issues in this earnings report isn’t something Hasbro can fully control, and that tariffs. In early February, the U.S. announced a new wave of potential tariffs, and Hasbro was quick to acknowledge it in its 2025 outlook, stating that it expects to feel the impact. The company manufactures the majority of its toys in China, and although it is trying to diversify its supply chain, any additional tariffs on Chinese imports would directly increase product costs.

To its credit, Hasbro has already factored some of this risk into its full-year guidance and is planning mitigation strategies, including supply chain adjustments and potential pricing actions. But if more tariffs are introduced later this year, or if political tensions escalate (since the guidance it’s already escalated), the company may not be able to offset the full impact.

Dividend and Valuation

Hasbro currently pays a forward dividend yield of 5.4%, with an annual payout of $2.80 per share. The payout ratio is just under 70%, which is reasonable, though it does limit flexibility for significant increases going forward. It’s also worth noting that the company hasn’t raised its dividend in five years, so this is not a dividend growth story.

From a valuation perspective, Hasbro doesn’t look expensive, but it is also far from being undervalued. The stock trades at a forward non-GAAP P/E of around 12.4x, slightly below the sector median and well below its own 5-year average of 17.9x. Other multiples like EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT also trade at a discount to Hasbro’s historical norms, yet still carry a premium to the sector median, especially on an EV/Sales basis, where the stock is priced at more than double the industry average. That tells me the market still values Hasbro’s strong brands and IP portfolio, particularly Wizards of the Coast, but is unsure about how quickly the core business can recover.

Overall, the valuation looks fair, but not compelling enough if we take into account all of the question marks about the future of this company and its ability to earn if the tariffs stick.

Conclusion

Hasbro is no longer trying to be everything at once, and that’s a good thing. The company is clearly working to become more efficient, more focused, and more aligned with what actually works: IP, games, and strategic licensing. But while the long-term strategy is appealing, the short-term perspective still feels too risky for me to initiate a new position here.

Declining toy revenues, ongoing restructuring, and renewed tariff risks do not allow me to give a bullish rating to Hasbro. At the same time, the company is making progress operationally, continues to generate solid free cash flow, and pays a decent dividend, which supports my neutral view.

I’m rating Hasbro as a Hold before Q1 earnings. Investors already in the stock may want to stick around to see if the turnaround continues, but for those only checking their options, I’d wait for either a clearer and possibly calmer macro environment or a more attractive entry point.

data.title: Valuations Then And Now: What History Teaches
data.description:
By Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Bradley Krom, & Brian Manby, CFA

April is recognized as Financial Literacy Month in the U.S., a time to empower investors with the knowledge needed to make more informed, confident decisions. One of the most misunderstood elements of investing is valuation: how much are you paying for a dollar of earnings, and is it worth it?

With tech stocks leading again, many investors are asking whether we're reliving the bubble of 2000. But a closer look at the data reveals a much different picture—and highlights global opportunities that may be overlooked without a solid grasp of valuation fundamentals.

The 2000 Tech Bubble vs. Today's "Magnificent 7"

Understanding valuation is a cornerstone of financial literacy. It's what separates hype from opportunity.

In 2000, companies like Cisco and AOL were trading at valuations that assumed unrealistic future growth—often with price-to-earnings (P/E) ratios north of 100.

Today's "Magnificent 7"—Apple, Microsoft, Nvidia and others—trades at an average forward P/E of 24. Elevated? Yes. But they also have the earnings to back it up. Over the past five years, they've delivered 24% annualized earnings growth, with forward expectations near 16%. That compares to the broader S&P 500, which trades at 21x earnings with growth closer to 13%.

The lesson: high valuations can be justified—but only if earnings growth is real and sustainable.

Figure 1: Price-to-Earnings of the "Magnificent 7"

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, S&P. *Calendar year price-to-earnings and earnings growth based on median analyst estimates. **Trailing 3-year where 5-year is not available. Growth is annualized. ***Estimated Long-Term Earnings Growth is annualized and based on median analyst earnings growth estimates over the next three years. Trailing 5-Year Earnings Growth and Estimated Long-Term Earnings Growth as of 3/31/25. All other data as of 4/7/25. Aggregate metrics in italics shown as weighted averages. You cannot invest directly in an index. See the glossary for definitions of terms and indexes.

International Equities: Still on Sale

Another key concept for investors to understand is relative value. Where can your capital potentially go further?

International equities are still trading at a 36% discount to the S&P 500, far wider than the historical median of 17%. Even after a recent rebound, there's room for international stocks to re-rate higher.

Take Japan, for example. Warren Buffett has built a substantial position in Japanese trading firms, which continue to trade at reasonable valuations (~9x–10x earnings) despite delivering strong earnings and dividend growth.

The WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ) is designed to capture similar exposure while reducing the risk of a weaker yen eroding returns—mirroring the currency-conscious approach Buffett employed by issuing yen-denominated debt.

Small Caps: Cheap but Stalled

U.S. small-cap stocks also offer discounts—but without a near-term catalyst. Tariff concerns, weaker earnings momentum and higher economic sensitivity have held them back.

While valuation metrics look appealing, this is a reminder that price alone doesn't equal opportunity. Investors must pair valuation with fundamental and macroeconomic awareness.

Investing Strategy or Portfolio Application

Valuation-aware investors may benefit from reallocating toward global strategies with more attractive pricing and structural support.

Consider these examples:

  • WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Fund (DEM): Offers an earnings yield of more than 6% and trades at a steep discount. It captures dividend-focused opportunities in regions where sentiment remains overly bearish.
  • WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ): Provides access to Japanese equities with strong fundamentals while managing currency risk.
  • U.S. multifactor and value-tilted strategies: Help mitigate valuation concentration in growth-heavy portfolios and reintroduce diversification across sectors and styles.

These tools offer diversified ways to balance growth potential with valuation discipline and make informed choices grounded in data, not headlines.

Making Sense of Market Signals

Financial Literacy Month can be used as a reminder that investing isn't about chasing trends; it's about understanding them.

Today's market isn't 2000. The "Magnificent 7" have real earnings behind their valuations. At the same time, undervalued international markets, especially in Japan and emerging economies, offer compelling alternatives.

A strong grasp of valuation principles can help investors navigate this environment with greater clarity and confidence. It's not just what you invest in, but why and how, that builds lasting success.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. Due to the investment strategy of these Funds, it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. Please read the Funds' prospectus for specific details regarding the Funds' risk profile.

DXJ: The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Investments in currency involve additional special risks, such as credit risk, interest rate fluctuations and derivative investments, which can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities and more sensitive to the effect of varied economic conditions. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers.

DEM: Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. Funds focusing on a single sector generally experience greater price volatility. Investments in emerging, offshore or frontier markets are generally less liquid and less efficient than investments in developed markets and are subject to additional risks, such as risks of adverse governmental regulation, intervention and political developments.

Brian Manby, CFA, Associate, Investment Strategy

Brian Manby joined WisdomTree in October 2018 as an Investment Strategy Analyst. He is responsible for assisting in the creation and analysis of WisdomTree’s model portfolios, as well as helping support the firm’s research efforts. Prior to joining WisdomTree, he worked for FactSet Research Systems, Inc. as a Senior Consultant, where he assisted clients in the creation, maintenance and support of FactSet products in the investment management workflow. Brian received a B.A. as a dual major in Economics and Political Science from the University of Connecticut in 2016. He is holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst designation.

Bradley Krom, U.S. Head of Research

Bradley Krom joined WisdomTree as a member of the research team in December 2010. Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Global Chief Investment Officer

Jeremy joined WisdomTree in May 2005 as a Senior Analyst, adding Deputy Director of Research to his responsibilities in February 2007. data.title: Labcorp Holdings: Regaining Credibility
data.description:
About a year ago, I believed that there was a healthier diagnosis for shares of Labcorp Holdings (NYSE:LH) with pandemic headwinds moving towards the background.

Shares had been trading around the $200 mark for quite a few years. The business was a modest beneficiary of the pandemic, which boosted demand for its diagnostics solutions. With testing revenues on the decline thereafter, amidst moving parts, the picture was a bit uneven, yet prospects for growth to return were seen in 2024, which made me believe that a valuation multiple re-rating could be in the works.

A Big Lab Player

In the spring of last year, Labcorp announced a bolt-on deal as it acquired the BioReference Health business from Opko Health (OPK). With this $237 million deal, Labcorp was adding about $100 million in clinical diagnostics and reproductive sales, with few other financial details being announced.

Labcorp claims to be one of the largest laboratory service providers in the world, performing hundreds of millions of tests per annum, offering thousands of unique tests for different purposes. The company mainly provides these services to large pharmaceutical names.

Early last year, the company reported a 2.5% increase in 2023 sales to $12.16 billion, with the OPKO deal in the spring of last year adding about a percent to sales. Some three quarters of revenues are generated from Diagnostics Laboratories, with Biopharma Laboratory Services making up a quarter of sales. With million of tests performed, this remains a very labor-intensive business, employing some 60,000 workers.

The company saw adjusted earnings down over three dollars to $13.56 per share, and worse is that GAAP earnings only came in at $4.04 per share, after no less than 9 adjustments made. Net debt was reported at $5.1 billion, ahead of the OPKO deal, for a leverage ratio around 2.5 times based on EBITDA of $2.1 billion.

The company guided for stabilization and recovery in 2024, seeing (adjusted) earnings up to $14.30-$15.40 per share, set to be supported by 5% sales growth. A $210 stock in the spring of last year, traded at just 13-15 times adjusted earnings, but as mentioned, these were adjusted earnings. Growth was set to be return with Covid-19 testing revenues on the decline only seen impacting sales by a percent, as the return of growth might result in valuation multiple inflation.

Modest Progress

Since April of last year, shares of Labcorp traded in a $200-$260 range, now trading at $220 per share, for very modest returns on an annual basis. This followed quite an eventful year, which included multiple bolt-on deals and solid organic growth having returned.

In April of last year, the company was the winning bidder to acquire selected assets of Invitae, a leading medical genetics business. The company paid $239 million for the assets, set to generate $275-$300 million in annual sales.

Based on this acquisition, the company trimmed the earnings guidance to $14.30-$14.90 per share alongside the second quarter earnings report, with the purchase of these assets being dilutive.

In September of last year, the company acquired a 15% minority stake in SYNLAB for an EUR 140 million valuation. This implicitly valued equity of the business at around EUR 1 billion, with SYNLAB being an EUR 2.64 billion revenue business.

In February of this year, Labcorp reported a 7% increase in 2024 sales to $13.01 billion, ahead of the original guidance for the year, aided by the impact of acquisitions announced during the year. Adjusted earnings rose by a dollar to $14.57 per share, in line with the revised guidance, as GAAP earnings improved dramatically to $8.84 per share, doubling compared to the year before with the earnings numbers looking a lot cleaner.

Net debt was down to $4.8 billion, down minimally on the year before, despite the impact of acquisitions, for a leverage ratio in the low 2s. The company guided for solid growth to continue in 2025, with revenues see up over 7% to $13.88-$14.05 billion, with adjusted earnings seen up around 10% to a midpoint of $16.00 per share, plus or minus forty cents.

Delivering

Given the solid growth reported in 2024, and guided for 2025, I think that appeal is increasing quite a bit. Trading at $220, shares trade at 15 times adjusted earnings reported in 2024, and 13–14 times adjusted earnings seen this year. This comes amidst modest deleveraging, with bolt-on M&A deals delivering on real growth.

The company continued with this trajectory, as Labcorp announced another deal with OPKO to acquire other assets of the BioReference Health subsidiary of OPKO. These include laboratory testing activities which focus on oncology and related testing, which contribute $85-$100 million in annual sales. The purchase price is pegged at $225 million, which includes a $32.5 million earn-out provision, with the remainder based on the upfront transaction price.

This again is another bolt-on deal, adding about 0.6-0.7% to pro forma sales, as this fits perfectly in the trajectory and strategy of the business here. Such moves are quite reassuring, as I like the steady focus on organic growth and bolt-on M&A here, amidst reasonable valuations.

All this makes me still rather upbeat on the business here, although that I have some long term concerns as well, driven by the emergence of AI. This could have real impact on some legacy testing methods, as this makes me a bit cautious with regard to the long term outlook for the business model.

Weighing it all together, I am constructive on the business and the valuation, yet fearful about the potential disruption of the business in the long run, and after performing the balancing act I am concluding that I think that shares are largely fairly valued here.

data.title: Azenta: Life Science Business Failing To Come To Life
data.description:
As shares of Azenta (NASDAQ:AZTA) have fallen to fresh 52-week lows, an update on the investment thesis is long overdue. In September of last year, I believed that the life science play was not coming to life, as this trend has continued to today.

Azenta might still be better known as Brooks Automation to long-term investors, as the business changed the name after the divestment of the semiconductor business, thereby becoming a pure play on life sciences. While this strategic reaction looked good on paper, Azenta has been struggling to show organic growth and post profits, leading to shares being down a great deal (albeit from an expensive start).

With a >10 times sales multiple of this business coming down to about 1 times right now, factoring in net cash holdings, expectations have come down, as the company maintains a strong balance sheet. For appeal to be seen, margin growth, and frankly real margins need to be delivered upon, as new management could unleash great value, provided it can deliver.

The Business

After Brooks Automation announced the sale of its semi business to private equity company Thomas H. Lee Partners back in 2021 in a $3 billion deal, the pure play life science business enjoyed real operating momentum. This, and enthusiasm among investors, sent shares up to levels above the $100 mark.

The remaining life science business generated about half a billion in sales, as peak valuations came in at $9 billion, including the net cash position of around $3 billion following the sale of the semi activities, but this still translated into demanding valuations by all means.

Not only was a 12 times sales multiple rather rich, it furthermore was applied to activities which were breaking even at best. Ever since then, the company has made very modest inroads. 2022 sales rose by 8% to $555 million, as adjusted earnings came in at $0.51 per share, these being highly adjusted numbers.

Sales growth accelerated 20% to $665 million in 2023, but this was all driven by dealmaking efforts (supported by the strong cash balances) in combination with share buybacks. On an organic basis, sales were actually down a percent, pressuring margins, with adjusted earnings down to $0.31 per share.

The company guided for 2024 sales up between 5% and 8% on an organic basis to $696-$718 million, and despite the promise of margin gains, adjusted earnings were seen down further to $0.19-$0.29 per share. Through the third quarter, the company announced the departure of its CEO as the company guided for 2024 now down to just $655 million.

With 53 million shares trading at $45 in September, and factoring in a $744 million net cash position, a resulting $1.7 billion operation asset valuation awarded activities a 2.5 times sales multiple. The issue is that of very modest and adjusted earnings, with realistic earnings largely non-existing.

Coming Down Further

Still a $50 stock in February, shares of Azenta have fallen to fresh lows of $25, which looks quite interesting, given the net cash position. In November of last year, Azenta reported a 1% fall in fourth quarter sales to $170 million, with full-year sales down 1% to $656 million as organic revenue growth was down 2%.

In all fairness, this was driven by a 27% fall in B Medical systems sales to $83 million. The company reported adjusted EBITDA of $49 million and adjusted earnings of $0.41 per share, up ten cents on the year before. These, however, remain very adjusted earnings, as the question can be asked if this business was still really profitable. The company aims to "fix" this by announcing the sale of the struggling B Medical systems business.

The company provided somewhat of a comforting guidance for 2025, seeing organic sales up 3-5%, to be accompanied by 300 basis point of EBITDA expansion, but in all fairness, the company has missed on its promises before.

In February of this year, the company reported a 4% increase in first quarter sales to $148 million, with the results no longer including B Medical Systems sales. While EBITDA from continuing operations rose by a full 4 points to 9% of sales, adjusted earnings were flat at $0.08 per share. This number compares to a $0.21 per share GAAP loss, as the discrepancy seems fair, relating to amortization, transformation and restructuring charges, but some of these are quite recurring and involve cash outflows as well.

Continued buybacks meant that the share count is down to 45 million shares, for a $1.12 billion equity valuation at $25 per share. This, however, includes a net cash position of $493 million, for an enterprise valuation of about $630 million. This values the business at around 1 times sales here, but the question is, of course, how profitable these sales are, or better said, can become.

What Now?

With net cash holdings down to $10 and change per share, the operating asset valuation of the firm stands at just around $15 per share, as this represents a life science business valued at 1 times sales, amidst no realistic profitability.

This makes it hard to get upbeat from a pure valuation and earnings potential point of view, as the business is breaking even at best here. That said, a solid balance sheet and the fact that sales multiples have compressed so much means that appeal can be found, but for this to happen, management must deliver on real margin expansion.

While the company anticipates modest advancements, (new) management does not have credibility on its side yet, but there is room for further disappointments, given the strength of the balance sheet. This makes me more upbeat now, purely from a valuation point of view, as the company has seen some operating momentum.

However, for shares to come to life, realistic earnings improvements are required, but the earnings numbers are quite murky. A speculative position might be warranted, based on the belief that management can deliver.

In that, I have no confidence to hold a full position, but right now, the opportunity is arising to take a speculative position here.

data.title: S&P 500: How Bad Could It Get? The Ultimate Worst-Case Scenario
data.description:
High uncertainty

The uncertainty is high, and nobody can really form any meaningful expectations about the near-term future, or even beyond the next 6-12 months.

Thus, it is important to understand all possible scenarios. The best-case scenario is the S&P500 (SP500) has possibly already bottomed out. The ultimate worst-case scenario is that the S&P500 could fall by 70% down to the 2000 level.

All scenarios are tied to the evolving tariff policy and the unfolding trade war, and specifically to the effects on growth and inflation, as well as the general financial stability. More broadly, the main issue is the unfolding process of deglobalization, and the reversal of the post-World-War 2 world order.

The best-case scenario

So, let's start with the best-case scenario. If the trade war de-escalates and the US and China engage in productive trade negotiations, with the hints of reduction in tariffs, the economy could avoid stagflation - inflation might not rise as much and growth could remain positive. Note, this scenario still assumes some tariffs in place, specifically the 10% universal tariff, plus the sector specific 25% tariffs.

It is important to note that over 40% of US inflation is shelter-related. How exactly are the tariffs going to boost rents? The rents are unlikely to be affected by the tariffs, which puts the ceiling on how high inflation could spike in the US, even if prices of some imports spike. Further assuming the global economic weakness, the price of energy could stay low, which should also limit the US inflation spike, despite the tariffs.

It is also important to note that the US is still suffering from labor shortage, which is likely to worsen with the tight immigration policy, in addition to the negative demographics. It's difficult to envision a deep recession without the spike in the unemployment rate. Even with tariffs, the unemployment in the US might not spike due to labor shortage. Thus, the US could avoid a deep recession, and experience only a mild recession or a low growth environment, with elevated inflation.

In this best-case scenario, the S&P500 has likely bottomed at the 4800-5000 level, although the volatility is likely to remain high.

The recession scenario with AI bubble burst

The US could experience a deeper recession, triggered by the negative wealth effect if the AI bubble bursts.

The S&P500 is heavily concentrated in tech mega caps associated with the Gen AI theme. The Gen AI theme is based on the heavy capex spending on data centers and other AI infrastructure/equipment. The first evidence of a reduced AI capex is likely to burst the AI bubble, and this could bring the S&P500 down to the 4000 level as stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) fall, and a deeper recession is triggered.

The recession with the credit event

If the unemployment rate increases, the housing market bubble is likely to burst and cause a credit event - in this scenario the credit spreads would widen as the financial sector experiences turmoil. The housing market has been frozen lately, as people who locked-in low interest rates during Covid refuse to sell, while the buyers remain on sidelines due to high mortgage rates.

The increase in the unemployment rate would force some people to sell into the weakening market, and trigger a steep price drop. Note, the current price-rent ratio is still on par with the 2008 bubble, as the chart below shows.

In this scenario, which also assumes a recession with the AI bubble burst, the S&P500 could fall to the 3000 level.

The worst-case scenario: Capital outflow

The previous scenarios assume that the trade war de-escalates, with some tariffs remaining in place.

The worst-case scenario assumes that the trade war escalates, where the reciprocal tariffs are imposed and trade negotiations are unsuccessful. Further, the US and China situation evolves into a complete separation between the two countries, with respect to flow of goods and capital.

Under this scenario there is a supply shock which could sharply boost the US inflation. In addition, the US economy would slip into a recession, and the AI bubble burst would accelerate. Higher interest rates due to higher inflation would also burst the housing bubble.

However, the key risk under this scenario would be capital outflow out of the US assets, as foreign investors, specifically the Chinese, sell their Treasuries (TLT). This situation could evolve into the downfall of the US Dollar (DXY) as foreign investors start questioning the US Dollar's role as a reserve currency. In this situation, the Fed could even be forced to hike interest rates to protect the Dollar, despite the recession.

The S&P500 could fall below 2000 in this situation, and this is the worst-case scenario - a deep recession, with capital outflow, and the bursting AI and housing bubbles.

Implications

Note, there is also a scenario where the policy narrative shifts to pro-growth agenda, before the negative effects of tariffs kick-in, and the trade war de-escalates. The bull market could continue under this scenario.

However, at this point the volatility is high because the worst-case scenario is unfolding. The bond market experienced a minor turmoil, which forced a temporary delay in the reciprocal tariffs. The bond market stabilized, but the US Dollar continues to depreciate, and this is worrisome.

The market understands that the delay in tariffs is temporary, and the negotiations between US and China have not started. In addition, there are no signs of meaningful progress in trade negotiations with other countries.

The AI bubble continues to deflate with Nvidia's China-related accounting write-off, and this is the early indication of coming next in the tech sector.

However, I am still optimistic that trade war will deescalate, and the volatility in bond market and the US Dollar will subside. But I do expect that the AI bubble is going to continue to deflate, with the reduction in AI capex. Thus, my target for S&P500 is around 4000.

Yet, the first signs that the US labor market is weakening with elevated inflation could start affecting the housing market, which increases the risk of a further drop in the S&P500 to the 3000 level as the housing bubble bursts.

I don't see it as likely that the US Dollar gets seriously challenged as a global reserve currency.
